findings.message: The infrastructure side still defines and publicly re-exports `NullReviewer`, even though the briefing says the infra `null_reviewer` module should be deleted after moving it to the `cli_composition` crate. This leaves an old composition-only adapter in the infra public API, contrary to the extraction boundary; remove the `pub use` and inline module from `mod.rs`.

The extracted `review_v2` surface is still largely untested. `run.rs` only covers the Claude path, and there are no direct happy-path/error-path tests for the Codex builder/run entry points or the new public helpers in `approved.rs`, `results.rs`, `scope.rs`, and `shared.rs`. That leaves the new composition boundary unverified and violates the repo requirement that public APIs have both success and failure coverage.

The moved `review_v2` modules still import the external `domain` crate as `domain::...`, but `cli_composition` already declares a root `mod domain` command module. That local module shadows the extern prelude, so paths like `use domain::review_v2::...` resolve to `cli_composition::domain`, which has no `review_v2`/`TrackId`, and the crate will not compile. Qualify the external crate as `::domain::...` or alias it across these new modules.

T003 adds a `libs/infrastructure -> cli_composition` shim dependency while T001/IN-02 already makes `apps/cli-composition` depend on `infrastructure`. That creates a Cargo dependency cycle, so the intermediate shimmed state cannot compile and the migration cannot be built as written.

T002 instructs the new public `CliApp` methods in `apps/cli-composition/src/lib.rs` to use `unimplemented!()` placeholders. That leaves panic-based code in a library crate and violates the repo's no-panics-in-library-code rule even if it is intended as temporary scaffolding.

T003 asks `libs/infrastructure` to add a `cli_composition` dependency for the re-export shim while D1/D6 require `apps/cli-composition` to keep a direct `infrastructure` dependency. That creates a Cargo package dependency cycle (`infrastructure` -> `cli_composition` -> `infrastructure`), so the staged migration cannot compile as written.

T006 step 3 says remaining `apps/cli/src` imports may be rerouted through `cli_composition` public API or `domain`, but D3/CN-01 reduce `apps/cli` to a single `cli_composition` dependency. There is no allowed final path that uses `domain` directly from `apps/cli`, so this instruction conflicts with the stated end state.

`T006` keeps `persist_commit_hash_for_track` in `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/mod.rs`, but `spec.json` AC-07 and the S6 summary both require that module to expose only adapter types. As written, the final acceptance target and the final task disagree on the end state, so the track cannot be completed without revising one side.

T006 asks for the final enforcement flip after removing `apps/cli`'s `infrastructure`/`usecase` deps and re-routing every remaining direct import through `cli_composition`, but the earlier tasks only define `CliApp` methods for the review_v2 family. The current `apps/cli/src` tree still contains other production imports (`track`, `verify`, `git`, `domain`, `pr`, etc.) and no task creates a façade surface for them, so the last step cannot be completed from this plan.

AC-07 says `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/mod.rs` will expose only adapter types after the move, but T006 explicitly preserves `persist_commit_hash_for_track` as a live free function in that module. Those two requirements disagree on the intended post-migration public surface.

`T005` moves `persist_commit_hash_for_track` out of `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/mod.rs` into `apps/cli-composition`, but `T006` still requires `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/mod.rs` to contain that same free function, and `spec.json` AC-07 says the module should contain only adapter types. The final state cannot satisfy all three; choose one location for the helper and update the conflicting task/spec entry accordingly.

`T005` reclassifies `persist_commit_hash_for_track` as a composition-layer helper and moves it into `apps/cli-composition`, but the symbol catalogue says `infrastructure::review_v2::persist_commit_hash_for_track` is a persistence helper that "Remains in infrastructure after D4 (IN-06)". The task is pointed at the wrong layer and the stated rationale does not match the documented symbol.

S3 says IN-06 is already complete after T003 because `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/mod.rs` then only re-exports adapter types, but S5/T005 still needs to move `persist_commit_hash_for_track` out of that same module. The plan's ordering is self-contradictory: either the module is already final at T003 or it still has outstanding work in T005.

T003 states that IN-06 is completed once `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/cli_composition.rs` is removed, but the same description also says IN-06 is only partially complete until T006. That conflicts with T005, which still needs to move `persist_commit_hash_for_track` before `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/mod.rs` can be adapter-only, so the step ordering is internally inconsistent.

T002 requires all new `CliApp` public API stubs in `apps/cli-composition/src/lib.rs` to call `unimplemented!()`, and T003 leaves those placeholders until T004. That would introduce explicit panic paths in non-test library code for intermediate commits that are also required to keep CI green, violating the repository's no-panics rule. Use fallible placeholder implementations such as `Err(...)` or defer exposing callable stubs until they can return real results.

T001 creates `apps/cli-composition` with `domain`/`usecase`/`infrastructure` dependencies and requires `cargo make ci` to pass, but the same task explicitly defers `deny.toml` changes until T006. Since D6/IN-08 say `deny.toml` must be updated to make `cli_composition` the allowed wrapper for infrastructure/usecase, the T001 intermediate state cannot satisfy the stated deny/sync checks while keeping CI green. The task order needs a CI-valid temporary policy step or a different sequencing of the crate dependency/enforcement changes.

The infrastructure catalogue marks `infrastructure::review_v2::persist_commit_hash_for_track` as `reference` and says it remains in infrastructure after D4, but T005 and AC-07 require moving it into `apps/cli-composition` so `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/mod.rs` exposes only adapters. This contradiction can let Phase 2 treat the function as retained while Phase 3 removes it, so the catalogue action should align with the implementation plan.

T002 instructs implementers to add public `CliApp` methods in the new `apps/cli-composition` library crate with bodies that call `unimplemented!()`, and T003 explicitly keeps those placeholders until T004 while still requiring `cargo make ci` to stay green. That conflicts with this track's required `No Panics in Library Code` convention, which forbids `todo!()` / `unimplemented!()` outside tests. The plan should use fallible stubs that return `Err(...)` or avoid introducing callable placeholders in non-test library code.

T001 creates `apps/cli-composition` with `domain` / `usecase` / `infrastructure` dependencies, explicitly defers `deny.toml` changes to T006, and still requires `cargo make ci` to pass. Since the ADR says `cargo make deny` enforces the hand-maintained wrapper policy and `cli_composition` must be added as a wrapper for `infrastructure` / `usecase`, this intermediate step is infeasible unless the `deny.toml` wrapper update moves earlier or the CI requirement is changed.

T003 adds `cli_composition` as an `apps/cli` dependency while requiring `cargo make ci` to stay green, but the plan does not update `apps/cli`'s `may_depend_on` until T006, where it changes directly to `["cli_composition"]`. D5 describes the needed intermediate state as `cli_composition + infrastructure + usecase`; without adding that earlier, layer verification rejects the T003-T005 state, so the staged migration cannot keep CI green.

T005/T006 require moving `persist_commit_hash_for_track` out of `libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/mod.rs` so the final module exposes only the listed adapter types, but the infrastructure catalogue still marks `infrastructure::review_v2::persist_commit_hash_for_track` as a `reference` item whose docs say it remains in infrastructure after IN-06. These artifacts give conflicting implementation instructions about whether the helper is kept or moved.

AC-04 says `apps/cli/Cargo.toml` should declare only `cli_composition` under `[dependencies]`, and T006 repeats that instruction. That is not executable as written: the ADR still keeps `apps/cli` as the clap parsing + emit binary, so it must retain external CLI/runtime dependencies such as `clap`, `serde`, etc. The acceptance criterion should narrow this to internal workspace/layer dependencies, removing only `usecase` and `infrastructure` while adding `cli_composition`.

CN-04 says the plan must not introduce a migration-period allow list, but the implementation plan requires exactly that: T001 adds `cli_composition` as an allowed dependent in `deny.toml`, T003 adds an intermediate `cli.may_depend_on = ["infrastructure", "usecase", "cli_composition"]` and a `cli_composition` wrapper for `cli`, and T006 later removes those intermediate allowances. As written, an implementer cannot satisfy CN-04 while following the required task sequence to keep CI green; either CN-04 should explicitly allow these intermediate policy updates or the tasks need a different migration strategy.

AC-10/AC-11 describe the final verification as confirming that `cli` direct dependencies on `infrastructure`/`usecase` are not rejected / not treated as layer errors. That contradicts AC-04/AC-05/AC-09 and ADR D6, which make `cli_composition` the only final `apps/cli` layer dependency and update `deny.toml`/`architecture-rules.json` so direct `cli -> infrastructure/usecase` dependencies are forbidden. As written, an implementer could satisfy these acceptance criteria by leaving or allowing the exact dependency edge this track is meant to remove.

`review local` used to call `resolve_track_id_for_write(args.track_id, &args.items_dir)` before dispatch, which anchored the write guard to the repository owning `--items-dir`. This now passes the raw optional track id into `CliApp::review_run_local`; that facade resolves/checks the branch from the process cwd and ignores `items_dir`, so running from another repo while pointing `--items-dir` at this track can record a review without verifying the target repo is on the matching `track/<id>` branch. Preserve the scoped write-resolution here and pass the resolved id to the facade, or make the facade resolve from `items_dir`.

`review classify` no longer pre-resolves the track id with `crate::commands::track::resolve_track_id(..., &args.items_dir)`. When `--track-id` is omitted, the new facade path resolves the branch from cwd instead of the repository owning `--items-dir`, so classification can use a track id from one repo while reading scope config from another `items_dir`. That can silently return the wrong scope mapping. Resolve the track id as before and pass `Some(track_id)` into `CliApp::review_classify`, or move the same `items_dir`-anchored resolution into the facade.

`review files` has the same `--items-dir` anchoring regression as `review classify`: the call now forwards `args.track_id.clone()` directly, so an omitted track id is resolved from cwd by the facade while file enumeration still uses `args.items_dir`. This can enumerate files for a different track than the one owning the requested `items_dir`, which breaks review scope selection. Pre-resolve with `resolve_track_id(..., &args.items_dir)` and pass the resolved id to the facade, or fix the facade resolver to honor `items_dir`.

`run_execute_local` resolves the track ID with `resolve_track_id_for_write` anchored to `args.items_dir`, but then delegates to `CliApp::review_run_local`, whose current implementation performs its own write branch check using cwd-based git discovery. That means a valid `sotp review local --items-dir <repo>/track/items` invocation can pass the intended items-dir anchored guard here and then fail against an unrelated current working repository before the review runs. The previous implementation did not re-enter a second cwd-based guard after resolving the track, so this regresses the documented items-dir anchoring for the local reviewer path.
